title: Coconut wax jar candle — lime and bergamot
subtitle: A bright, zesty candle with a clean, fresh throw
excerpt: A jar candle made with coconut wax, scented with lime and bergamot essential oils. Coconut wax has a very white finish and holds fragrance extremely well; it gives a stronger cold throw than soy wax at the same fragrance load.
sourceNotes: Coconut wax pour temperature: 52 to 57°C, lower than soy container. Lime essential oil flash point approximately 46 to 52°C; this is close to or below the pour temperature and requires careful handling. Add lime at the coolest possible pour temperature (52°C) and confirm with your specific oil's flash point data sheet. Bergamot flash point typically 58 to 65°C, which is above the pour temperature. National Candle Association fragrance load notes; coconut wax supplier technical documentation.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 jar candle (~200 ml, approximately 30 hours burn time)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally per jar. Confirm lime essential oil flash point before use; if below 52°C, use bergamot only or substitute with a lime fragrance oil with a higher flash point.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 24 hours before lighting. Two weeks of cure is recommended for coconut wax, which can take longer than soy to fully bind with fragrance.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store away from sunlight and heat. Coconut wax holds fragrance well; the citrus notes will fade after 18 to 24 months but the bergamot base persists. Keep the lid on when not in use.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a fragrance or essential oil can ignite near a flame. Lime essential oil has one of the lowest flash points of common candle-making oils; always check the data sheet for the specific batch you are using.
The first burn of a jar candle, which establishes the melt pool size and prevents tunnelling on subsequent burns.
The rest period after pouring during which fragrance binds to the wax. Coconut wax can take longer than soy to fully cure; two weeks gives the best throw.

content.text: Coconut wax produces a very white, creamy candle with a smooth surface and excellent fragrance throw. It holds essential oils and fragrance oils at a higher load than soy wax, and the cold throw (the scent when unlit) is noticeably stronger at the same fragrance percentage. The practical consideration with this blend is lime essential oil: its

can be close to or below the coconut wax pour temperature of 52 to 57°C, depending on the specific oil. Check the supplier's safety data sheet before use. If the flash point is 52°C or above, add the lime at the lower end of the pour temperature window (52°C). If the flash point is below 52°C, use a bergamot-only formula or switch to a lime fragrance oil with a declared flash point above 60°C. The combined fragrance load here is 7% of wax weight. Two weeks of

items.prepNote: 3.8% of wax weight; sharp citrus top note. Check flash point data sheet before use.
3% of wax weight; floral-citrus mid-base note
Sized for a 200 ml jar; CD-12 as a starting point

content.text: Warm the jar. Secure the wick to the jar base and thread through the centring bar.

Melt the coconut wax in the pouring pitcher over low heat. Do not leave unattended. Coconut wax has a lower melt point than soy; it liquefies quickly. Remove from heat once clear and molten. Let the wax cool to 52 to 55°C on the probe thermometer before adding essential oils. At this temperature, add the bergamot first, stir to combine, then add the lime. Pour promptly.

Pour into the prepared jar, leaving a 1 cm gap. Do not disturb for at least 6 hours; coconut wax can take longer to fully set than soy. Top up any sink hole. Leave to cure for at least 24 hours before lighting, and ideally 2 weeks for the best
Trim the wick to 5 mm. Burn until the
is complete with the melt pool reaching the jar edge. This takes 3 to 4 hours for a 200 ml jar.
Store away from sunlight and heat. Coconut wax holds fragrance well; the lime top note fades before the bergamot base. Keep the lid on when not in use.
